title: Distinguished Colloquium: Jeff Dean, February 10, 2026
text:
good afternoon. Um,
it's not 4:30 yet, but since the room is
already full, I'll just start doing
introduction to save some time. Okay. Um
it's a real pleasure to introduce Jeffin
a chief scientist for Google uh research
and the Google deep mind and also he's a
co-lead of Gemini project.
I would say before the now famous Jeff
Dean at Google era, I actually met Jeff
in the 90s when Jeff was in digital
western research and so I can confirm is
not overnight success story. Jeff has
been quietly except exceptional for a
very long time. Um
Jeff's bio you probably read in the
flyer um reads a little bit like a
guided tour of modern computing and uh
from largecale information retrieval
operating system distributed system
compilers and then if that wasn't enough
the second career is that AI machine you
know ML accelerator vision model
low-level software
>> I'm going to not upgrade Chrome right
now [laughter]
and um
and also um
distillation neuronet network uh
architecture search large language
multimodel uh model and application
ranging from chip design to healthcare
translation and so on. So one of my
favorite details of Jeff uh is that he
did his undergraduate thesis on neuronet
networks
and which means two things I think.
First he worked on neuronet network
before
uh it was cool and second he has the
rare distinction being able to say I
like neuronet network before Google
made it popular. Okay, maybe Jeff did
that to himself. But um if you're
wondering what takes um uh to to be good
to have a good research uh taste,
apparently the answer is start early to
be Jeff. So
uh along the way Jeff has received the
ACM prize in computing ite
medal. He's a ACM fellow, a member of US
National Academy of Engineering and uh
American Academy of Arts and Sciences.
So, we're very fortunate to have him um
to give us the distinguished lecture
today. Please join me to welcome Jeff.
[applause]
>> All right. [applause]
Thank you very much for that kind
introduction and for hosting me. I'm I'm
unexpectedly in town and Kai said, "Hey,
you want to come by and give a talk?"
So, I'm delighted to be here. Um, and
I'm going to talk to you about, you
know, what's happening in AI? I don't
know if you've noticed, but it's a thing
these days. Um, and we have many people
at Google working on this uh area. So,
I'm presenting the work of not just my
work, but many many people at Google uh
and elsewhere.
So, a few observations which may not
come as a huge surprise to you. So
machine learning and AI have really
changed our expectations of what is
possible with computers. You know if you
think back 1015 years ago you know
computers couldn't see very well. They
couldn't really understand speech all
that well. They definitely couldn't
understand language very well. And now
we have tools that enable computers to
interact with people in a much more
natural style because they understand
the modalities that people understand
and want to communicate with. Um and
along the way increasing scale of larger
amounts of compute, larger amounts of
data and more larger scale models have
delivered better results nearly
continuously for the last sort of 13 or
14 years. This has been the trend and
seems to be holding up even even today.
Um
in addition to scale, algorithmic and
sort of model architecture improvements
have also provided massive improvements
as well. And these two things multiply
together, right? If you can have much
larger scale and much better model
architectures that are able to learn
more, you know, per floating point
operation, those things together mean
instead of, you know, 20x from scale,
you have 20x from scale and 50x from
algorithms and you end up with a
thousandx better thing than you had not
that long ago. Um, also the kinds of
computations we want to run and the
hardware in which we want to run them
are changing dramatically. So four
observations which are maybe not
surprising. Uh first neural nets and
gradient descent. These are seem to be
key building blocks for how do you
actually build truly intelligent uh
computer systems. Um and they're not new
ideas. They're things that have been
around for a long time. They were
invented in the 70s and 80s. Um and
there was actually a lot of excitement
about them in the late 80s and early
90s. I first ran across them in a you
know intro lecture where I was
introduced to neural networks roughly
like this. Um you know we have this kind
of nice very abstracted away uh version
of how we think real life neurons behave
where you have a bunch of inputs you do
some processing and you decide are you
going to fire or not and what strength
are you going to fire um and you have
connections of these things. Um, and
then back propagation is a way is a
really nice algorithm that enables you
to adjust the weights on the edges of
all these uh connections in order to
make uh sort of um the the model behave
more in the way that you want it to
behave where you give it a bunch of
examples and back propagation enables
you to make the the model learn uh the
behavior you want.
So I saw these as an undergrad in 1990
and I got super excited and I said,"Oh
wow, that seems like a really good
abstraction. Um, it feels like it's a
trainable thing and it can learn almost
anything." Um, and at that time we were
not using very large uh neural networks
because we had really pathetically wimpy
computers. Um, but I said, "Oh, maybe if
we use the parallel computer in the
department, I could we could train much
bigger neural networks. That would be
great. Maybe we just need to use 32
computers instead of one and then we
could train really impressive neural
nets. That would be nice." So, I did it.
I asked the professor who introduced the
neural networks topic in one of the
classes I was taking if I could do a
senior thesis with him, Vipin Kumar. Uh,
and um, he said, "Sure." So I played
around with two different approaches for
training neural networks in parallel.
One is uh this is what you would now
call model parallel and data parallel
training. Uh but I called them pattern
partitioned and pipelined approach. Um
and uh these speed up curves are
interesting but they taper off because I
did the stupid thing. I didn't make the
model bigger and bigger as I increase
the number of browsers. I just made it,
you know, much harder to parallelize uh
with a fixed size model. Um but I sort
of filed that away as like oh that's an
interesting abstraction and it seems
like something I should pay attention to
but then I ignored it for you know the
next 25 years.
Um, okay. So now I'm going to give you a
whirlwind tour of a b b b b b b b b b b
b b b b b b b b b b b b b b b b b b b b
b b b b b b b b b b bunch of advances in
the field of ML that have happened in
the last 15 years that are really all uh
you know combined together to produce
today's you know most sophisticated
models. Um how did these today's models
come to be? Well, it was through
advancements like the ones I'm going to
describe. [snorts]
So, the origin of my getting back to
working on neural nets uh at Google was
I bumped into Andrew Ing who's a
Stanford faculty member and I in one of
our micro kitchens and I said, "Oh, what
are you doing here?" He's like, "I'm not
sure yet." Uh because I just started a
week ago. Um but my students at Stanford
are doing uh interesting things with uh
training uh neural networks for speech
and vision. I'm like, "Oh, that's cool.
We should train really big neural
networks." I said, "Uh, and so that was
the origin because we actually had quite
a lot of computers in those days." And I
said, "Oh, why don't we try training
very very large neural networks?" Um,
and so we built a software system that
enabled us to express neural network and
connectivity of those neural networks
and do both model and uh data parallel
training across uh many many computers.
Um and that enabled us to train neural
networks that were 50 to 100 times
larger than what we the largest ones we
could find anyone talking about. Um
and uh we uh had a a way of doing this
where we had um actually many many
asynchronous replicas of the model uh
cop getting updates of what the current
parameters of the model were you know
churning away on their local batch of of
examples computing a gradient and
sending it to this distributed set of
parameter servers in order to update the
parameters of the model. This is because
it's asynchronous definitely not the
right mathematical thing because in the
meantime the model parameters have moved
from the other replicas giving the the
parameter servers updates to the
parameter model to the parameters. Um
but it seemed to work so we were sort of
happy anyway despite it being sort of uh
theoretically wrong. Um,
and one of the first things we we
trained was a very large model to do
completely unsupervised learning on 10
million random YouTube frames. Um, and
interestingly, we had a kind of a a
locally connected u neural network for
computer vision um that would use an a
reconstruction loss. So we try to take a
high level representation of the neurons
at the highest level of the model and
then try to reconstruct the pixels that
have been uh given as input and um
because that bottleneck layer uh forced
the model to sort of learn higher level
abstractions uh than the actual raw
pixels. Um, what we ended up finding was
at the top level of this this model,
some of the neurons had learned to be
sensitive to whether or not there was a
face of a cat in in the image. Some of
them learned to be sensitive to whether
there was a face. Some were sensitive to
the, you know, is this the outline of a
person. Um, all without being told any
of these images contained any of those
kinds of objects. So it's uh kind of
neat to see that you could learn these
high level abstractions and
representations from a purely
unsupervised objective. Um what we could
then do is use the initialization of
this unsupervised training in order to
train with a supervised method um to uh
uh try to do computer vision uh image
classification. And we used the
unfortunately we used the 22,000
category version of imageet which is a
more thinly traded version than the 1000
category one that most people are most
familiar with. Um but we got a 70%
relative improvement in imageet 22k
state-of-the-art by initializing with
our unsupervised uh model and then doing
supervised learning on the um you know
the training set for that for that data
set which is quite a large relative
improvement. So we were happy with that
and we s saw that scale really mattered.
Um we also started to do a bunch of work
in using neural networks for for
language tasks. And one of the things we
found was that even relatively simple
models where you have a vector-based
representation of words or phrases um so
that you have a nice you know thousand
or five 500 dimensional representation
of each each word or token um
and have relatively shallow models. So
in fact we had just the embedding vector
and then we would use the embedding
vector of the word to try to predict
nearby words. That was one of the
approaches we tried. Uh and what we
found was that if you apply this to lots
of text uh and use this training
objective, you end up with nearby words
uh in the highdimensional space tend to
be related ones. So like cat and puma
and tiger are all low distance in the
highdimensional space. And also that
directions are meaningful. Uh so you you
you go in the same direction to change
the gender of a word in a gendered
language uh as uh regardless of what
word you start with. King and queen
versus man and woman.
Uh a year or two later uh my three of my
colleagues Ilia Sutzgver, Oral Vignyals
and Quaclay uh this uh used a neural
encoder using an LSTM to uh essentially
be able to learn an encoder for one
sequence and then a decoder for a
different sequence. Um, and when you
initialize the state of the LSTM by
running the encoder, that ends up sort
of capturing the things that have
occurred in that input sequence and
enables the model to then use that
initial state to then do a good job of
predicting the the target sequence. Uh
so the the canonical example use case of
this is language translation where maybe
you have a bunch of English French
sentences uh pairs that mean the same
thing and the input sentence is English
and so when you're training you try to
decode the French sentence and you get a
supervised learning objective for every
token or for every word and then when
you're trying to translate a new
sentence you just use the encoder to
encode the rep the LSTM state and then
decode from that state in order to
produce the translated uh French
sentence.
So as we saw more and more success of
neural networks and in particular
scaling up neural networks for problems
in computer vision, speech recognition
and language, we were starting to
both get excited but also a bit worried
about how we would actually deploy these
much better models. Um, so this is kind
of the origin of how we started to
create our own tensor processing unit
chip uh program is I did this back of
the envelope calculation and said okay
we have this really nice high quality
speech recognition model. It has the
error rate for speech recognition which
is a huge improvement. It's sort of
equivalent to 20 years of speech
research in like six months since we
started the project by training just a
very large you know particularly
sophisticated model architecture. It's
like a eight layer fully connected
neural net uh just trained on a lot of
data. And that back of the envelope
calculation said if we wanted to deploy
this to a scenario where we had a
billion users talking to this model 3
minutes a day, we would need to double
the number of computers Google had. And
that didn't seem very tenable for
rolling out a a better speech
recognition system. Um so that really
convinced us that exploring
uh you know much more specialized
computational devices for doing neural
network inference and later neural
network training was going to be a a
fruitful path. Um and so in you know
this sort of bootstrap got us to really
approach that with conviction and
started up a chip design team in order
to build these these chips. And then the
first chip emerged two years later as
hardware projects tend to do. They take
a little while but we eventually
produced the TPUv1 chip um which had
took advantage of two really nice
properties of of neural networks. The
[clears throat] first is that
reduced precision is fine especially for
inference. Um so uh you're perfectly
happy with you know about seven or eight
bits of precision not 16 or 32 or 64 as
most people are thinking about in terms
of comput floatingoint computation
things. Uh and also that nearly all of
the models we were exploring were made
up of different combinations of a
handful of specific kinds of uh linear
algebra operations. you know things like
um matrix multiplies are at the heart of
them and then certain kinds of
dotproducts and normalization and
scaling. But if you think about most of
the machine learning algorithms of
today, different rearrangements of those
kinds of operations mean that you can
build an accelerator that is capable of,
you know, very high performance reduced
precision linear algebra and that can be
applicable to a pretty wide range of
neural networks. And it's much more
efficient because that's all it needs to
do. It doesn't need to do anything else.
It doesn't need to run Microsoft Word or
Chrome or anything else. Uh and so TPUv1
turned out to be 15 to 30x faster than
contemporary CPUs and GPUs and 30 to 80
times more energy efficient uh than
contemporary CPUs and GPUs. Um and it's
also now the most cited paper in ISK's
50-y year history.
Uh
so we then uh turned the chip design
team's attention to the training problem
because uh we felt like being able to
scale up training more than we were able
to on on CPUs and GPUs was going to be
important. And so this is where the uh
focus has been for for quite a while.
And we've now produced many many
generations of TPUs that are essentially
uh machine learning supercomputers,
right? there uh it's more than just a
single chip unlike TPUv1 instead it's
lots and lots of chips connected with a
custom high-speed interconnect um that
enables us to you know distribute model
training over those chips and
communicate uh both uh activations and
parameter updates and gradient updates
across that that custom interconnect.
Um, version four introduced this funky
thing where we have an optical uh
interconnect for racks of these
accelerators. So, there's a rack of 64
accelerators and we can make it seem as
though that rack of 64 accelerators is
right next to another rack of 64
accelerators even though they're, you
know, 100 meters across the data center
floor. And if that rack fails, we can
swap in another rack and make it seem as
though this rack is now right next to
that one in terms of the net topology.
[snorts] Um,
which is important. As you get more and
more scale, you end up with failures.
And so you want to be able to
reconfigure things like that. [snorts]
And so we've seen, you know, continual
uh hardware performance scaling. So if
you look at TPUv4 here on the left
column, you know, the peak flops per
chip is about 275 teraflops. You know,
the max pod size was uh 40 4,96 uh uh
chips and now we're sort of at roughly
double that pod size and a much much
more HPM memory and much more memory
bandwidth and many more flops per per
chip. Uh if you compare the performance
peak performance per pod which is sort
of the largest network configuration we
have of the the custom interconnect the
TPUv2 pod is 1x and the most recent uh
sort of v6 but now we've stopped
numbering them sadly it's harder to keep
track of but Ironwood is the sixth
generation of FTPUs is about 3600
times the performance per pod FTP v2 and
it's also much more energy efficient So
about 30x better uh energy uh you know
flops per watt which is what you
ultimately care about in terms of u sort
of energy cost.
The other thing that has happened in the
last 15 years is open source tools have
really enabled the whole community to
build using the same pieces of
abstraction and and software libraries
and the community can work on sort of
improving those tools as well. So we
developed a a series uh a software
called TensorFlow based on our initial
lessons from the sort of 2011 to 2014
era with an internal uh software library
that we were using to to parallelize
computations across um you know many
computers and train large models and and
do inference. Um and so and then more
recently uh various people at Meta and
other elsewhere uh introduced PyTorch
and then uh another group of people at
Google have introduced a more functional
uh style paradigm uh computation called
Jax. [snorts]
Um all of these are are great systems
and you know we have been using Jax for
our Gemini training uh and it's been
great.
Um and then in 2017 a number of my
colleagues uh uh worked on uh coming up
with a better architecture than the sort
of recurrent LSTMs that were kind of the
the state-of-the-art models for language
uh at the time. And the observation they
had was really in an LSTM you you have a
vector of state uh uh of sort of the
current representation of the state and
then when you advance one word at a one
token at a time you update that state
and there's a sophisticated uh thing
that uh sort of updates the state and
then you go on to the next word and you
update the state again and so as you
progress through say a hundred words of
a sentence you update the state a 100
times. Um, and there's two problems with
that. One is you have this sequential
process. Every word you have to wait for
the up update of the state and there's a
sequential dependency on that state
being ready before you can go on to
update the state for the next word. So,
it's not very parallelizable. Uh, which
is not good on modern hardware. And the
other thing is you're trying to force
all the things you went through into a
single vector that you have all the all
the things you might want to remember
from the things you went through uh in a
single vector rather than just saving
all the vectors you went through and
then being able to look at them uh as as
you wish uh later in the sort of
language modeling process.
So the key observation in the
transformer model is let's save all
those vectors or some representation of
those vectors and then pay attention to
them in a way where we have a learnable
attention mechanism.
[snorts]
And what they were able to to show was
that compared to LSTMs of one, two or
four layers in red there, uh the test
loss on the y-axis um went down
considerably using a transformer. and
and no matter what model scale you
looked at. So sort of a 10 to 100x less
compute and 10 times smaller model gave
you uh with a transformer would give you
roughly the same quality as as you would
get with an LCM that's much larger and
much more computensive.
In 2018, you know, I think people really
started to take seriously the idea,
well, I guess in the word devec work, we
were already sort of doing that. But in
2018, really looking at how could you do
language modeling at scale with
self-supervised data and just the
observation there's a lot of text in the
world. And so self-supervised learning
on this text can give you almost
unlimited amounts of training data where
you know the right answer, right? like
you have text and then you hide the next
word and you try to guess the next word
and if you guess it correctly that gives
you get a sort of a one and if you guess
it wrong that gives you a clear error
signal that you can propagate into
improving the model so that uh in such
settings you will guess better next time
and so this is really one of the major
reasons that these chat and language
models have gotten uh yeah I created a
custom example for this week [laughter]
Um,
and so there are two kinds of training
objectives that people typically use.
One is called auto reggressive where you
get to look at the prefix everything to
the left uh and try to predict the next
word. So Princeton blank, you know, that
that word is a little bit hard to
predict. Could be Princeton New Jersey,
could be Princeton University. Um, you
know, Princeton University blank. That
one's hard to predict as well. Uh, is
cold in the blank. that that BV is a
little easier to predict. Um so, uh
that's auto reggressive and then fill in
the blank uh is where you get to have a
training objective where you just hide
you get to look in both directions and
you hide some of the words and you try
to force the model to guess the missing
words uh that you've hidden. And again,
you get this nice uh sophisticated um
you know uh very very crisp uh error
signal that you can use to train the
model. And you can just train on lots
and lots of text either e either of the
objectives. Um the fill-in- thelank one
tends to be used to build
representations that some that give you
a good representation of a bunch of
text, but you can't really use it for a
chatbot because you don't have the
things to the right. So uh you have to
sort of end up using mostly auto
reggressive models for conversational
applications but fill and blank also has
some good uses.
Um in 2021 a number of other of my
colleagues uh applied transformers to
computer vision. At that time the sort
of state-of-the-art was to use various
kinds of convolutional models which have
these kind of local receptive fields and
then you build up and share the
parameters among different positions in
that uh in the U image. Um and instead
you can just apply a transformer uh
based model to the same same problem.
And what they found was again you get a
much better you know if you look at the
TPU core days to reach certain levels of
accuracy um the TPU core days are much
lower and the accuracy is higher uh
compared with say ResNet 152 which was
kind of a good state-of-the-art model at
that time.
And the other nice thing is you can kind
of see what the attention mechanism is
paying attention to. So it gives you a
little bit of model interpretation of
like what is it paying attention to in
order to make a say a classification
prediction that that middle one is an
airplane.
[clears throat]
Another area that we have done a bunch
of work in including myself is in sparse
models. Uh you know in a dense model you
have a bunch of parameters in the model
and every inference or every token you
activate the entire model in order to
make the prediction for for that
example. Um, and that that seems kind of
uh that's not how our brains work,
right? We have very different pieces of
our brains that are useful for different
kinds of things. And we activate the
right part. So while I'm, you know,
worried about the garbage truck backing
up at my car, some parts of my brain are
active and the part that thinks about
Shakespearean poetry is not active.
So spar with sparse models the idea is
we want to have different pieces of the
model that are good at different kinds
of things. Um and then be able to
automatically learn not just those
experts but also learn the routing
mechanism to learn which pieces of the
model to actually activate for those
different examples. And so it gives the
model much larger capacity but still
keeps the inference cost relatively low
because you're only activating, you
know, a small number of the parameters
in the model. A and importantly, it
gives you an 8x reduction in training
compute for the same accuracy. That's uh
choice A there. Or you could choose to
spend the same amount on training the
model
>> [snorts]
>> uh and [clears throat] get a much better
model, right? Same amount of compute.
That that would be choice B there. And
often these kinds of things you you
choose a bit of A and B and end up kind
of somewhere there. Um but this has been
a really important tool for making our
model better and better over time is
having sparity be part of the mix. Uh
there's a whole litany of work that uh
both I and so and my colleagues have
done in this general area looking at
various aspects of how to do the routing
you know how many experts do you
activate um and so on.
>> [clears throat]
>> Another thing we've been working on is
building up uh abstractions for
distributed ML computations. So,
Pathways is a system we started working
on quite a while ago that really wants
to abstract away this pile of
accelerators underneath your computation
and give you the sort of researcher the
illusion that you just have a giant
computer with tens of thousands of chips
attached to it and you can mostly just
focus on the machine learning
computations you're trying to express.
And underneath the covers, Pathways does
a bunch of work to manage how to
communicate uh the machine learning
computation you've expressed uh amongst
all these chips. And so it will for
example use within one of these pods, it
will use the custom TPU interconnect.
When you go across the pod boundary in
the same data center building, it will
use the data center network. uh when you
go across buildings in the same data
center campus, it will use the purple
network link. And when you go go across,
you know, perhaps widely distributed
metro regions, it will use the um sort
of wide wide area network uh links.
And [clears throat] then when you
actually put jacks on top of pathways,
what that means is that we can drive the
entire training process from a single
Python process on one host. So that that
Python process says, "Oh great, I have
access to you know these these many
thousands of TPU chips and um I can run
this computation and pathways kind of
deals with what happens when different
pieces of the system fail uh and and
swapping in new parts uh new new
hardware under the covers.
Uh, another thing that tends to happen
when you run at increasingly large scale
is sadly not all chips or machines or
network cards or cables work as
designed. That's a very sad but true
state of the world. Um, and in
particular, some of them don't just fail
in ways that you can just like detect
them immediately and and replace them.
they sometimes non-deterministically
sort of produce incorrect results um
silently and sometimes that's related to
dynamic conditions like the temperature
of the rack at that particular moment.
Um, this is really challenging when
you're running independent compute on
these things, but it's really worse when
you're trying to have all these chips
perform as a single orchestrated uh, you
know, machine learning uh, training
computation where everything is is
synchronous and this can quickly spread
bad results. So like if one of these
things flips one of the bits in the
exponent field of your gradient all of a
sudden you'll get you know 10 to the
20th propagating as a gradient instead
of 02 and that makes you very very sad.
[clears throat]
Um
and so we monitor during the training
process all kinds of metrics about the
health of our model. And in particular,
you can look at, for example, the norm
of the gradient at every layer of the
model and see are you seeing spikes in
that. So here's an example of a spike uh
due to one of these silent data
corruption things. Um now not all spikes
are necessarily due to these kinds of
machine issues. Here's another spike.
There is no SDC. This is just some
particular batch of examples that
contain something that caused a very
large gradient uh to occur. Um, and it's
a little hard to discern those things.
Um, so one of the ways we do this is we
have deterministic replay that we
trigger automatically. So if we see a
gradient spike, then we will effectively
replay that step or the last few steps
and see if we get the same answer. And
if we get the same answer, we conclude,
well, that's probably okay. That was
probably due to the data, not due to
hardware errors. But if the hardware
errors are uh you know causing the issue
then hopefully we get a different answer
the next time
we replay it.
And you can also have cases where we
don't get a gradient spike
uh but there is actually silent data
corruption happening um but it doesn't
